intro: Transitioning from TypeScript to Scala shifts development from structural type-checking on top of JavaScript to a robust functional and object-oriented architecture on the JVM. Engineers make this migration to leverage Scala's sophisticated type system, advanced pattern matching, and high-performance concurrency frameworks for data-intensive or highly parallel backends. This change replaces build-time type erasure with real compile-time validation, immutability by default, and monadic error management.

diffs.from: Structural static typing that is stripped away during the build process, leaving an unvalidated JavaScript runtime layer.
diffs.to: Static nominal type system with powerful structural inference, generalized algebraic data types (GADTs), implicits, and opaque types.

diffs.from: C-style notation relying heavily on bracketed structures, explicit semicolons, arrow assignments, and interface properties.
diffs.to: Expression-oriented, concise layout combining functional patterns, traits, and infix operators with optional curly braces in modern versions.

diffs.from: Volatile memory graph managed by browser or host runtime generational garbage collection routines.
diffs.to: Managed JVM heap optimization, running high-throughput garbage collectors tuned for multi-threaded object life cycles.

diffs.from: Single-thread event architecture driven by microtask queues and native Promise abstractions.
diffs.to: Multi-threaded concurrency leveraging native Futures with ThreadPools, or purely functional effect models (Cats Effect, ZIO).

diffs.from: Minimal core package framework necessitating heavy reliance on external NPM module dependency trees.
diffs.to: Extensive, highly optimized collections framework built on native functional transformations, immutable structures, and parallel processing streams.

examples.title: Pattern Matching over Algebraic Data Types
examples.description: Contrasting a TypeScript type discriminated union check with an exhaustive, type-safe Scala pattern match over case classes.
examples.toCode:
sealed trait WebResponse
case class ErrorResponse(message: String) extends WebResponse
case class SuccessResponse(data: String) extends WebResponse

def handle(res: WebResponse): String = res match {
  case ErrorResponse(msg) => msg
  case SuccessResponse(_) => "Data loaded"
}

examples.title: Asynchronous Processing Sequences
examples.description: Converting Promise operations into Scala Future structures requiring explicit thread contexts.

tips.title: Enforce Immutability via Case Classes
tips.body: Ditch mutable variables and type instances. Model all structural data patterns inside Scala using immutable 'case class' declarations and modify values using '.copy()'.

tips.title: Replace null and undefined with Option
tips.body: Scala explicitly models the presence or absence of data through the 'Option' (Some/None) monad, which avoids unexpected runtime exceptions.

tips.title: Utilize for-comprehensions for Chaining
tips.body: Swap complex Promise chaining routines for Scala's clean 'for-comprehension' syntax, which flattens nested asynchronous operations into readable steps.

tips.title: Migrate Build Chains to sbt
tips.body: Exchange front-end package ecosystems like Webpack or NPM for Scala's standard build tool (sbt), which handles compiler plug-ins and JVM dependency tracking.

faqs.question: Can Scala code target front-end web platforms directly?
faqs.answer: Yes. Scala.js compiles Scala codebases directly into highly optimized JavaScript scripts, allowing teams to share types and business logic between backends and frontends.

faqs.question: What replicates the TypeScript 'any' escape hatch inside Scala?
faqs.answer: Scala uses 'Any' as the root class of its type hierarchy, but using it breaks compile-time safety. Use generics or type classes instead.

faqs.question: How do Scala traits differ from TypeScript interfaces?
faqs.answer: While both define structural contracts, Scala traits can contain fully concrete, executable method implementations and fields, allowing safe mixin composition.
